terms.definition: The hand-designed construction of new model input columns from existing fields using domain rules, arithmetic, datetime logic, or aggregates—distinct from learned feature extraction.
A new column computed deterministically from one or more source columns, such as a ratio, difference, aggregate, or binned category.
A column capturing combined effect of two or more inputs—commonly a product or conditional flag—so models can represent non-additive relationships.
A column extracted from timestamps: calendar parts (hour, weekday), elapsed durations, seasonality flags, or cyclical sin/cos encodings.
A problem-specific reshaping of a column—log, clipping, unit conversion, or business-rule flags—motivated by how the target relates to raw measurements.
The broader practice of preparing model inputs; feature creation is the subset where columns are explicitly designed rather than learned by an extractor.
Mapping continuous values into ordered buckets (bins) to expose threshold effects or reduce noise from fine-grained measurements.
Representing periodic fields like hour or month with sine and cosine pairs so adjacent values (23 and 0 hours) are numerically close.
Elapsed time since a domain event—last purchase, signup, or login—often predictive in churn and recommendation models.
A summary statistic computed over related rows—count, sum, mean—joined back to the modeling grain, typically via groupby.
